records.ety: [OE. spire, spir, a blade of grass, a young shoot, AS. spīr; akin to G. spier a blade of grass, Dan. spire a sprout, sprig, Sw. spira a spar, Icel. spīra.]

defs: A slender stalk or blade in vegetation; as, a spire grass or of wheat.
quotes: An oak cometh up a little spire.

defs: A tapering body that shoots up or out to a point in a conical or pyramidal form. Specifically (Arch.), the roof of a tower when of a pyramidal form and high in proportion to its width; also, the pyramidal or aspiring termination of a tower which can not be said to have a roof, such as that of Strasburg cathedral; the tapering part of a steeple, or the steeple itself.
quotes: A spire of land that stand apart, Cleft from the main.
Tall spire from which the sound of cheerful bells Just undulates upon the listening ear.

defs: A tube or fuse for communicating fire to the chargen in blasting.
The top, or uppermost point, of anything; the summit.
quotes: The spire and top of praises.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Spired (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Spiring.]

defs: To shoot forth, or up in, or as if in, a spire.
quotes: It is not so apt to spire up as the other sorts, being more inclined to branch into arms.

records.ety: [L. spira coil, twist; akin to Gr. <?/<?/<?/: cf. F. spire.]

defs: A spiral; a curl; a whorl; a twist.
The part of a spiral generated in one revolution of the straight line about the pole. See Spiral, n.
